summary: Atwood revisits the double-click usability problem by highlighting a little-known Windows feature that maps all actions to single clicks. Introduced in Windows 98, this option transforms the desktop experience to follow web conventions, where items are underlined like hyperlinks and hovering selects them. He urges readers not to dismiss Microsoft's attempt to solve this fundamental UI inconsistency, suggesting the solution deserves more consideration than it received.
key_points: Windows 98 introduced a single-click mode that eliminates the need for double-clicking
The feature uses web metaphors (underlining, hover-to-select) to create consistency
Microsoft made a genuine attempt to solve a real usability problem that went largely unnoticed
money_quotes: Don't be too quick to dismiss Microsoft's effort to solve the double-click problem.
It's an attempt to map everything to the single mouse click, using the web metaphor.
Note the subtle selection behavior to accommodate this change; hovering your mouse over an item now selects it.
key_insight: Microsoft actually tried to fix the double-click usability problem in Windows 98, but the solution was buried in settings and largely forgotten.
